data.title: KCE: An Attractive Investment Option For Lofty Returns As Capital Markets Poised To Mark New Records
data.description:
SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF (NYSEARCA:KCE) appears to be a worth considering investment option for investors seeking to generate lofty returns both in the short and long-term. The ETF’s portfolio exposure to fundamentally strong companies in key financial industries, such as asset management & custody banks, investment banking & brokerage and financial exchanges & data, increases its potential to generate a solid price appreciation and dividend return. In addition, these three industries are poised to benefit from improved trading, IPO, venture, M&A and investing activities. Its holdings' average annual earnings growth estimate of 15% over the next 3 to 5 years appears strong enough to fuel price and cash returns. Therefore, I initiate coverage of KCE with a buy rating.

Capital Markets Outlook Signals Robust Growth

KCE’s capital markets portfolio is spread across three industries: asset management & custody banks, investment banking & brokerage and financial exchanges & data. The recent performance and outlook of companies in these industries demonstrate strong prospects for an uptrend in 2025. KCE’s top 5 holdings: Robinhood Markets (HOOD), Interactive Brokers (IBKR), LPL Financial Holdings (LPLA), The Goldman Sachs (GS), and Morgan Stanley (MS) - belongs to investment banking & brokerage industry. All these companies generated robust growth in the fourth quarter, with expectations for record-breaking numbers in 2025.

In particular, trading platforms are expected to witness high growth as rising stock markets, lower rates, and economic growth increase investors' confidence. In addition, trading platforms strategy of expanding their global foot-prints by permitting clients across the world to trade and conduct transactions contributed to the growth trend. This trend is expected to continue because trading platforms aggressively seek to capture more international clients by launching new products and offering a user-friendly platform.

For example, Robinhood Markets, the online trading and investment platform, saw a share price growth of more than 400% in the last twelve months amid its blistering growth rates. The company reported 115% year-over-year revenue growth in the December quarter, while its net income surged 10X year over year to a record $916 million. The revenue growth was driven by a substantial increase in trading volumes, subscribers, and deposits. Vlad Tenev, CEO and Co-Founder of Robinhood, believes that the company has a significant growth potential in 2025 because of its strategy of enabling anyone across the world to trade and make financial transactions.

Similarly, Interactive Brokers share price soared 127% in the past twelve months due to the robust financial and operational growth. Its fourth quarter revenue was $1.39 billion, compared with $1.14 billion in the same period last year. The adjusted earnings per share of $2.03 jumped from $1.52 in the year ago period. In Q4, the number of its customer accounts increased 30% year over year to 3.34 million, thanks to the robust international clients growth. Moreover, the company expects its revenue and earnings to increase at healthy pace in 2025, given a gigantic increase in its customer accounts and new product launches.

Whereas, with diversified business models, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, delivered solid share price and financial growth. Morgan Stanley’s net revenue of $16.2 billion grew from $12.9 billion in the year-ago period. The company saw growth across its businesses. Its institutional securities revenue increased 47% year over year, while the wealth management and investment management revenue increased at a double-double rate. Wall Street expects Morgan Stanley’s revenue and earnings to increase at a high single-digit rate in 2025 while Goldman’s earnings are expected to grow at a double-digit rate.

Whereas stocks from asset management & custody banks in KCE portfolio, such as Northern Trust Corporation (NTRS), The Bank of New York Mellon (BK), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), Ares Management Corporation (ARES) and Victory Capital Holdings (VCTR), are flourishing in easing monetary condition and steady economic growth. Shares of The Bank of New York Mellon rallied 60% in the past twelve-month on the back of record revenue and earnings. In 2024, the bank delivered record revenue of 18.4 billion and its earnings per share increased 18% year over year. The bank expects to extend its growth momentum, while Wall Street estimates 14% earnings growth on a mid-single-digit revenue increase.

Ares Capital Management and Victory Capital Holdings continued to generate solid returns for investors due to impressive financial and operational growth. In 2024, Ares Capital saw a record fundraising of $92.7 billion and its fee-paying AUM grew of 12% year-over-year. The company's management anticipates that improving transactions and significant pent-up demand is likely to drive its financial growth. Victory Capital Holdings share price doubled in the past twelve months on record financial metrics, with expectations for further growth in 2025 due to Amundi acquisition and ETF expansion.

Companies in the financial exchanges & data industry, such as Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), S&P Global (SPGI), Moody's Corporation (MCO), Nasdaq (NDAQ) and Morningstar (MORN) continued to generate a steady growth. After significantly surpassing adjusted earnings per share growth expectations in 2024, the S&P Global expects its adjusted revenue growth of 5%-7% in 2025 and adjusted diluted EPS to hover around $17.00 and $17.25 compared $15.70 per share in 2024.

Overall, it appears that capital markets are poised to accelerate the growth trend in 2025 because of improved market fundamentals. Therefore, investing in capital markets using investment options like SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF could be a great strategy.

KCE Characteristics Support a Buy Rating

SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF generated an exceptional total return of 84% since 2023, outperforming the S&P 500’s return of 57%. Its robust share price momentum is supported by its diversified and well-balanced portfolio of 63 fundamentally strong companies. While the portfolio offers diversification to three important industries in the financial sector, which we already discussed above, a well-balanced weight in each stock increases its potential to capitalize on the broader trends and lower risk factor. KCE’s top 10 holdings account for 19.5% of its entire portfolio, with a top holding making up 2.5%.

The fund's strategy of weighting stocks based on their fundamentals instead of a market-cap contributes to its share price momentum. For instance, Robinhood, the top-performing financial stock with strong fundamentals, is its largest holding. In addition, the majority of its top holdings belong to investment banking & brokerage industry, which is experiencing record-breaking financial and operational growth. Stocks from the asset management & custody banks and financial exchanges & data industries account for 43% and 22% of its entire portfolio.

According to the fund’s data, its portfolio has an average annual estimated earnings growth rate of 15% over the next 3 to 5 years. I believe double-digit earnings growth is sufficient to drive the share price upside momentum and cash returns. The ETF currently offers a dividend yield of 1.52%. The fund lifted its dividend by 16% in the trailing twelve months.

In addition, KCE's healthy earnings growth rate continues to balance its price to earnings ratio despite a solid share price growth in 2024. Its stock is currently trading around 17x forward PE. Its expense ratio of 0.40% is down from the median of all ETFs and in-line with most of its peers. With $555 million in assets under management and 61K in average shares trading volume, KCE’s liquidity factor is also strong enough to make it a worth considering option for the long and short-term investing. The ETF earned a buy rating with a high score of 4.40 based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. KCE is ranked at a third spot in its sub-asset class.

Risk Factors

The financial sector carries one of the highest volatility factors because of its correlation to external factors like monetary and economic conditions. A tighter than expected monetary policy and lower than expected economic growth can negatively influence the ETF’s performance. Its beta of 1.33 and high standard deviation of 18 also signals that the ETF can fall faster than the broader market index in downtrends.

In Conclusion

With the robust recent performance and solid outlook, investing in the capital markets could be a great strategy. In addition, investment vehicles like SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF offer a targeted exposure to fundamentally strong stocks in the capital markets. Its well-balanced portfolio of 63 stocks positions it to fully capitalize on the growth potential while lowering the risk factor. The ETF's cheap valuation, healthy dividend yield and low expense ratio also makes it a worth considering investment option.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Inches Away From Record High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 ended its two-week skid, finishing up 1.5% from last Friday. The index is now just inches below its record close from January 23rd, 2025 and is up 4.19% year-to-date.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index over the past 5 days:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

As of February 14th, the U.S. Treasury put the closing yield on the 10-year note at 4.47% and the 2-year note at 4.26%. See our latest Treasury Snapshot here. ETFs associated with Treasuries include: iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), and iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

Here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above its 200-day moving average since November 1st, 2023. For the past few weeks, the index and its 50-day moving average have been crossing each other, with the index currently ahead.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. I've also included a 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. On December 18, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (3.45%) since November 30, 2022 (3.59%).

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 4.19% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 3.52% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cisco Q2: Strong AI Infrastructure Order Growth
data.description:
In my previous article in November 2024, I assigned a ‘Buy’ rating to Cisco (NASDAQ:CSCO) (TSX:CSCO:CA), highlighting its product order recovery with strong AI growth. Cisco delivered an accelerated revenue growth in Q2 FY25, driven by their AI initiatives. I reiterate a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $75 per share.

Strong AI Infrastructure Order Growth

Cisco released its Q2 FY25 result on February 12th, 2025, reporting 9.4% revenue growth and 8% adjusted EPS growth, as shown in the chart below. The growth acceleration was driven by strong AI business growth and robust product order growth. Cisco secured more than $350 million in AI infrastructure orders during the quarter. During the earnings call, the management expressed strong confidence in exceeding more than $1 billion of AI infrastructure orders for FY25.

I think the biggest highlight for the quarter was the strong product order growth. As breakdown in the table below, the total product order grew by 29% year-over-year, and 11% organically without the contribution from Splunk. During the quarter, Cisco experienced strong order growth in the Service Provider & Cloud market, driven by increasing demand for network infrastructure and AI workloads.

Another highlight from the quarter was the company’s aggressive shares repurchase activities. During the quarter, the company returned $2.8 billion in total, including $1.6 billion in dividends and $1.2 billion in shares buyback. The Board also approved another $15 billion for shares repurchase, bringing the total authorization to $17 billion. The total authorization accounts for almost 6.6% of total market capitalization, based on today’s stock price, which is quite notable.

Outlook and Valuation

For FY25, Cisco is guiding for $56 billion-$56.5 billion in revenue, with $3.68-$3.74 in adjusted EPS. As indicated during the earnings call, the current guidance reflects the impact of tariffs from China, Canada, and Mexico. They have been adjusting their supply chain to mitigate the potential impact from tariff, which has already reduced the overall exposure by 80% so far.

For the near-term growth, I am considering the following factors:

  • Networking: The Networking product orders have been growing at double-digits, driven by switching, enterprise routing and other IoT solutions, as noted during the earnings call. The management anticipates their 800 gig Nexus switches will continue to grow, fueled by strong AI workloads and data center growth. For the near-term, I forecast the Networking business will grow by 6% annually, in line with the overall switch and routing market growth.
  • Security: Cisco has a strong product portfolio in advanced data management, analytics, and threat detection. As discussed in my previous articles, I think Cisco needs to invest more in its Security business to better compete with other companies such as Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and CrowdStrike (CRWD). I assume Cisco will grow its Security business by 4% annually, aligned with its historical average.
  • Service: The segment growth has been quite consistent due to its business nature. I assume the segment will grow by 5% annually, comprising 3% price growth and 2% contract wins.
  • Putting together, I forecast Cisco can grow its revenue by 5% organically. Additionally, I assume the company will allocate 5% of revenue toward M&A, contributing 170bps to the topline growth.
  • I model 10bps margin expansion, driven by the reduction in SG&A expenses. I calculate the total operating expenses will grow by 6.5% annually, leading to 10bps margin expansion.

The WACC is estimated to be 8.8% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 0.8; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 5%; equity $234 billion; debt $31 billion; tax rate 18%.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future FCF, the fair value is calculated to be $75 per share, as per my estimate.

Key Risks

During the earnings call, the management announced that Gary Steele, the former CEO of Splunk, will leave at the end of the quarter. As a result, Cisco is seeking an internal or external candidate to lead Splunk. I think the CEO resignation is not a sign for the integration of a newly acquired business. It is unclear whether the management change will have any strategy impacts on Splunk’s business in the future.

Conclusion

I think Cisco is on the right track with its investments in AI infrastructure business, as reflected in the strong order growth. The stock price is undervalued; therefore, I reiterate a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $75 per share.

data.title: Novartis: Strong Execution With A P/E Discount
data.description:
It was a good momentum to suggest increasing Novartis' (NYSE:NVS) (OTCPK:NVSEF) position. Since our mid-December 2024 release, the company's stock price is up by almost 10% (Fig 1). In our last assessment, despite checking Novartis's headwinds from Kisqali patent litigation and Entresto competition, our buy rating was supported by clinical advancements and increased mid-term sales guidance support. Following the spin-off of its eye care business (Alcon) in April 2019, and its generics unit (Sandoz) in October 2023, Novartis is a pure-play innovative medicines business focused on oncology, immunology, neuroscience, and cardiovascular conditions. Key growth drivers include higher investments in Research & Development and strategic early stake acquisitions. Last week, the company communicated another acquisition. In the meantime, Novartis also reported its Q4 results, so this is a good time to review our investment thesis.

Fig 1

Q4 Results and Our Positive Take

The company delivered a very strong set of results in Q4 2024, with sales 3% ahead of consensus expectations. Novartis' key drivers were Entresto, Kisqali, Kesimpta, and Sandostatin (Fig 2), offsetting Cosentyx and Zolgensma's weak performance. Going down in the P&L analysis, the company's operating costs SG&A and R&D were broadly in line with expectations, allowing an outperformance at the core operating profit level (10% ahead of consensus). Q4 net sales grew by +16% (accelerating from FY 2024 results of +12%). In detail, Q4 turnover was helped by a 3% tailwind from rebate true-ups. What is impressive is the fact that Novartis is not only accelerating its growth sales trajectory but also its margin. On a full-year basis, the company's operating income grew +55%, with a core EPS up by 24% to $7.81.

Source: Novartis Q4 results presentation - Fig 2

Novartis also communicated guidance significantly ahead of market expectations. The top-management team targets top-line sales to grow mid-to-high single digits and core operating profit to grow high single to low double digits. On the FX, Novartis expected an impact of -2% and -3% on 2025 sales and EBIT (Fig 3).

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

  1. This week, Novartis communicated the acquisition of Anthos Therapeutics Inc. This is a privately held clinical-stage biotech company with a lead asset called abelacimab. This drug will be used for the prevention of stroke and systemic embolism in patients with atrial fibrillation. There is an upfront payment of $925 million, up to $2.15 billion. Abelacimab is being trialed in FDA Phase 3. The anticoagulation market is worth approximately $20 billion, suggesting a 10/15% potential market share penetration; this drug could reach sales of $2-3 billion. In FDA phase 2, Abelacimab proved to reduce the incidence rate of major or clinically relevant non-major bleeding events. Overall, our team sees the acquisition of abelacimab as a minor positive. But we are more impressed by the Novartis top-management team to transform the company into a truly innovative player.
  2. The company anticipates a modest impact from the Medicare Part D redesign, with longer-term benefits possible from better compliance. Additionally, the outlook assumes Promacta, Tasigna, and Entresto US generic entry in H1 2025. Therefore, the company feels confident in delivering a plus 5% CAGR in sales, even with generic competitors. We also note that Novartis settled with generic company MSN Pharmaceuticals Inc. on ongoing Kisqali patent litigation. We anticipated these risks in our previous assessment; this was our take: this negative risk-reward from the Kisqali patent litigation ruling has a low probability but material (10-15%) share price downside risk. More importantly, we anticipate a better product MIX, which will drive operating profit margins to 40% by 2027.
  3. Even if we report the pelacarsen cardiovascular readout delay from H1 2025 to H1 2026, Novartis has various late-stage trial readouts (Fig 6). The company's 2025 catalysts include 1) proof of concept for Votoplam (Huntington's) and 2) Ianalumab (HS).
  4. Last time, we reported how the Swiss National Bank (SNB) unexpectedly cut rates by 0.5%. Following the Q4 printout, the company increased its dividend per share to CHF 3.50 from CHF 3.30, an increase of 6.1% (Fig 4). Local pension funds, dividend investors, and ultra-high-net-worth individuals (UHNWIs) always seek to buy a Swiss dividend player that combines growth and value. As a reminder, in July 2023, the company communicated a $15 billion share buyback to be executed in 2.5 years. There is a $5 billion buyback to be completed. With an FCF above $15 billion (Fig 5), even accounting for DPS uplift, the company has ample margin for M&A and future buyback.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Fig 6

Earnings Changes and Valuation

The company's medium-term top-line sales target is one of the lowest-risk profiles within its large-cap peer group (Roche, Sanofi, and AstraZeneca). The company has a broad product development pipeline to help offset competition such as from Cosentyx biosimilars (in 2029) and Entresto generics (in 2025). This, combined with operational performance and new investments in radiopharmaceuticals and cell/gene therapy areas, will likely support fundamental value creation. Following the FY 2024 sales and considering the negative FX evolution from Fig 3, we slightly increased our sales to $53 billion. Post Investor Day, we continue to anticipate an EBIT margin of 37.8% ($20 billion). This not only assumes a strong commercial execution but a lower impact from generics competition. Following the strong financial performance in Q4, Novartis has margin leverage upside with a forecast EBIT margin of 40%, which is not included in our base case scenario. Following higher sales, we raise our EPS estimates by 4% to CHF 7.8 ($8.6).

Going to the valuation, we continue to see an upside in Novartis shares. Applying an unchanged P/E set at 14.5x (aligned with its 5-year historical average - Fig 7), we derive a target price of CHF 113 ($125 in ADR). However, we should note that Novartis does not trade aligned with its sector median P/E. This is due to historical reasons and conglomerate discounts. However, we should consider Novartis a “pure-play” innovative medicines business and value the company accordingly (so the upside might be higher).

Fig 7

Risks

We have long-standing coverage of Novartis, so we report 1) lower-than-anticipated top-line sales growth, 2) negative FX evolution, 3) negative impact from patent expiration with lower margins, and 4) pipeline failure and worse commercial success for critical launch assets. Novartis' cash generation is strong, and it has a solid balance sheet; however, the company faces reimbursement and pricing challenges. Additionally, we should report downside risks from higher interest rates, regulatory risks, and the inability of the company to reach its medium-term margin targets.

Conclusion

Compared to the past, there is M&A optionality and a rich pipeline. Despite solid near-term sales and EPS growth, Novartis trades at 12.2x 2025 core EPS. With the downside protection of a juicy CHF dividend yield and an ongoing buyback, the company is a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 02/16/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Week Ahead: RBA To Begin Easing Cycle And USD Downside Correction May Be Nearly Over
data.description:
The US threat of aluminum and steel tariffs and reciprocal tariffs initially shook the markets, but the implementation at some future date gives the impression that the threats are negotiating ploys helped take away the sting. In his first term, Trump converted some of the tariffs to quotas, and that one scenario for the likely outcome now. Still there is great uncertainty of the US intent and strategy. For its part, the greenback finished last week lower against all the G10 currencies but the Japanese yen. It is not coincidental that US rates also fell last week. Despite the upside surprise to US CPI and PPI, many concluded that CPI was particularly noisy, and the inputs into the PCE deflator suggests the Federal Reserve's targeted measure may slip. The Fed funds futures finished the week with greater confidence of a cut by the end of Q3 than it did before the inflation gauges were reported. Still, the dollar's pullback seems more corrective than a trend reversal and the tariffs still loom on the near-term horizon.

The lack of visibility given what still seems to be an unpredictable US administration means that short-term market participants do not have much conviction. The bilateral talks between the US and Russia over Ukraine is a bit like the Suez Crisis (1956) where US interests diverged starkly from the UK and France. Now may be the most strained relationship between the US and Europe since then. In a generally light economic calendar in the week ahead, the preliminary February PMI stands out. Canada and the UK report January CPI, and nine different Fed officials are scheduled to speak. The Reserve Bank of Australia is widely expected to begin its easing cycle with a quarter-point move, while the Reserve Bank of New Zealand will likely maintain its aggressive course with its third consecutive 50 bp rate cut.

US

Drivers: There are two dominant forces. The first is divergence. The next Fed cut was fully discounted for July, but after the second consecutive decline in US unemployment, sticky inflation, and indications suggesting the economy continues to grow above trend, despite the disappointing retail sales report. The second is the US tariff threats. Given the role of US multinationals, their foreign direct investment strategy, and their significant role in US imports, tariffs will disrupt them. Nevertheless, the market is still inclined to buy US dollars on tariff threats and sell dollars when the threat subsides or looks more like a negotiating tactic.

Data: There is a full slate of data but nothing that has the heft to materially impact expectations for the trajectory of Fed policy. The general line from most Fed officials, including Chair Powell and Governor Waller, who some see as a possible successor to Powell, have indicated that provided the economy and inflation evolve as expected, there is still scope for lower rates. No fewer than nine Fed officials speak in the week ahead. The potential supply shock if tariffs (and retaliation) will be taken serious by the Federal Reserve as it learns from the experience with the pandemic. The minutes from last month's meeting will likely underscore the Fed's patience. January housing data (permits/starts, existing house sales) will draw some attention, while the February surveys (Empire, Philadelphia, and preliminary PMI) may pose little more than headline risk. The Fed funds futures market now has the next cut fully discounted in October, and around a 50% chance of a second cut before the end of the year.

Prices: Despite the higher-than-expected CPI and PPI, the US two- and 10-year yields finished lower last week. It was the fifth consecutive weekly decline in the 10-year yield and the fourth weekly decline in the five weeks for the two-year yield. The Dollar Index fell to a two-month low near 106.80. Stale long dollar positions are being cut amid disappointment that the so-called Trump trade is not working. The next technical target is around 106.35. A break of 106.00 could target the 104.90-105.15 area.

EMU

Drivers: The ECB is widely expected to cut rates at least two more times before the Federal Reserve cuts again. The policy divergence weighs on the euro. Also, Europe still seems ill-prepared to address the powerful challenges posed by Russia, China, and now the US. France and Germany have offered to send troops to Greenland, and even if their purpose is not clear, it is not due to Russia's aggression, or China's wolf diplomacy, but apparently it is a response to the US threat. The US-Russia peace talks appear to shift a large burden to the Europe and many suspect this coupled with increased defense spending, will boost the supply of longer-term European debt.

Data: With Q4 GDP already reported, the December trade and current account data, typically not market movers, are unlikely to draw much attention. New information in the form of the Germany's February ZEW survey and the preliminary February PMI will likely attract more interest. Recall that Germany's IFO survey saw a small improvement in the assessment of the overall business climate and the current assessment, but the expectations component fell. Lastly, the average EU tariff on US imports is about 3%, which is about twice the average US tariff on EU imports.

Prices: Short covering lifted the euro to $1.0515 last week. While it is a new high for the February, the January high was slightly below $1.0535. The euro has not closed above $1.06 since November 12, a week after the US election. The $1.0550 area corresponds to the (38.2%) retracement of the downtrend from late September, the last time it traded above $1.12. Still, we view these euro upticks as corrective in nature rather than a trend reversal.

China

Drivers: Many observers see China as the largest beneficiary of the disruption emanating from the new US administration, despite the tariffs and export restrictions. The Trump administration is not even a month into the four-year term and the tensions with Mexico, Canada, and the EU have rarely been higher. Trump's territorial ambitions and refusal to rule out force to take Greenland and the Panama Canal makes China's wolf diplomacy or Russia's aggression in central and Eastern Europe seem less like an outlier. The average US tariff on Chinese imports is higher than the average tariff China imposes on US goods, though there are other market-distorting practices. The PBOC daily reference rate for the dollar shows Beijing is still resisting the downside pressure on the yuan.

Data: China reports new and existing house prices. There are some preliminary signs that China's housing market has begun stabilizing. The prime rates will be set this week but there is no reason to expect a change yet. January FX settlements will also be reported. They have fallen sequentially from December since 2020. In the ten years before the pandemic, FX settlement typically increased in January.

Prices: Rather than decline in the face of US tariffs, offshore yuan has appreciated for the past two weeks and four in the past five. The greenback finished the week near CNH7.25. The January low was closer to CNH7.2345 and the 200-day moving average is near CNH7.22. Over the course of last week, the PBOC set the dollar's reference rate between CNY7.1706 and CNY7.1719.

Japan

Drivers: There are two chief drivers of the exchange rate. First, the yen remains sensitive to the direction of the 10-year US Treasury yield. On a rolling 30- and 60-day basis, the correlation between changes in the exchange rate and changes in the US 10-year yield is higher than the correlation with changes in the 10-year interest rate differential. Second, the yen appears sensitive to speculation about the timing of the BOJ's next hike. Currently, the swaps market has about a 50% chance of it in June and 80% in July. Surveys of economists find a preference for July. Judging from the record of the last BOJ meeting, renewed weakness of the yen could influence the timing.

Data: Japan reports Q4 GDP. It looks to be around the same pace as Q3 24 (~1.2% annualized), but the composition looks different. Consumption may be weaker, and inventories look like a drag. That will be offset stronger business investment and a contribution by net exports. January trade figures are also due. Japan runs an overall trade deficit but has a bilateral surplus with the US of about $72 bln in 2023 and $63 bln in 2024. The January national CPI is due, and the Tokyo report a few weeks ago points to firmer price pressures. Japan will see the preliminary February PMI, but the local market does not seem to react much to the report. That said, the composite PMI has risen for three consecutive months through January and the 51.1 reading is the highest since last September.

Prices: On the back of the rise of US rates in the first half of last week, the greenback recovered from the dip below JPY151 on February 7 to JPY154.80 after the "hot" CPI, but with rates falling following the PPI, and then the disappointing retail sales, the dollar returned to almost JPY152 ahead of the weekend. For the second consecutive week, the dollar finished below its 200-day moving average (~JPY152.70). It may take a break below 4.40% in the US 10-year to push the dollar below JPY152 again. The low from early December was near JPY148.65.

UK

Drivers: So far, the UK appears to have escaped being singled out by the Trump administration. Some suspect, the US will seek to extend the UK's distance from the EU. Prime Minister Starmer had made better relations with the EU an important policy objective. The risk is that the government will try to stay in US good graces and improve relations with the EU and fail to do either. Meanwhile, changes in sterling against the dollar and sterling's changes against the euro are correlated 0.80 over the past 60-day. A six-month peak was recorded in late January near 0.85.

Data: Contrary to the Bank of England's warning of a contraction in Q4, the UK economy eked out a small expansion in Q4 24. The UK has a busy economic calendar. Market-sensitive reports include the latest employment figures, January CPI, retails sales, and the flash February PMI. Given the rate earlier this month and the forward guidance, barring a significant surprise, a cut at the next meeting (March 20) will remain unlikely. That means that the Bank of England will have more data before the next "live" meeting in early May. The swaps market is confident of another rate cut then.

Prices: Sterling had fallen 10% from the late September high (~$1.3435) to the mid-January low (~$1.21). With last week's gains, it met the (38.2%) retracement target near $1.2610. The (50%) retracement and the 200-day moving average are found in the $1.2765-90 area. The December high was slightly higher (~$1.2810). Sterling settled firmly and the five- and 20-day moving averages are trending higher. However, the momentum indicators are getting stretched and sterling settled above its upper Bollinger Band for the first time since last September.

Canada

Drivers: The postponement of the US 25% tariff on Canadian imports sparked the recovery of the Canadian dollar. Canada is the single biggest source of US aluminum and steel imports, and the 25% tariffs would be in addition to the general tariff. The uncertainty of what happens next month may deter a further recovery of the Loonie as month-end approaches. At the same time, the US two-year premium over Canada narrowed slightly from the recent peak near 165 bp to around 153 bp, while the 10-year premium narrowed from the record high near 150 bp on February 4 to about 137 bp ahead of the weekend.

Data: Canada reports January CPI. In Q4, Canada's CPI was flat at an annualized pace after falling 0.8% in Q3. The underlying core measures averaged 2.45% in December, down from 3.35% in January 2024. This would be the most important input for the central bank, but the tariff threat is now more salient, the market believes. Canada also reports December retail sales. They are likely to have recovered from the flat November, which was flattered by auto sales; without which retail sales fell by a sharp 0.7%. December portfolio flows are due as well. In the first 11 months, net purchases by foreign investors averaged C$16.4 bln a month. In the same period in 2023, inflows averaged C$2 bln a month. During the week, Canada reports housing starts and existing home sales, which typically do to elicit much of a response in the foreign exchange market.

Prices: Since the greenback's spike to almost CAD1.48 on February 3 when US tariffs seemed inevitable, it has pulled back by around 4.35% to reach CAD1.4150 ahead of the weekend. The dramatic position unwind sent the US dollar to its lowest level since mid-December. The US dollar had rallied by around 10.2% from the late September low (~CAD1.3420) to that early February high. The CAD1.4100 area corresponds to the (50%) retracement objective. We suspect the move is nearly over, however. The momentum indicators are stretched, and the lower Bollinger Band was frayed before the weekend. On the topside, previous support near CAD1.4260 may now offer resistance.

Australia

Drivers: The Australian dollar has suffered disproportionately in the US dollar's broad rally since the end of September. The Australian dollar fell near 12.5% from the late September high near $0.6940 to the early February low, slightly below $0.6090. Australia runs a modest bilateral trade surplus with the US (~$17 bln) but so far has remained out of the US crosshairs. Leaving aside Japan, where the BOJ is tightening monetary policy, the Reserve Bank of Australia has the only G10 central bank that has not cut interest rates, and that is about to change.

Data: The RBA meeting on February 18 is arguably the most important event in the week ahead, which also features the important employment report, and the preliminary PMI. The futures market is discounting about an 87% chance of a quarter-point cut, while the Bloomberg survey found 28 of 32 respondents also project a 25 bp cut (also around 87%). The Reserve Bank of New Zealand meets the following day (February 19). The RBNZ easing cycle began last August with a quarter-point cut, but then slashed its cash rate by 50 bp in the last two meetings of 2024. The swaps market is pricing about a 90% chance of another half-point cut, which would bring the cash rate target to 3.75%. Since August 1, 2024, the New Zealand dollar has been the weakest among the G10 currencies, depreciating by almost 3.6%. The Australian dollar, in contrast, is off about 2.2% over the same period.

Prices: The Australian dollar has recovered from the spike low on February 3 slightly below $0.6090 to approach $0.6370 before the weekend. It shrugged off the disappointment that at least one Trump advisor is wary of granting Australia an exemption for its metal exports to the US. Ahead of the weekend, the Aussie took out the January high (~$0.6330) and surpassed the upper Bollinger Band (~$0.6345). The $0.6415 area corresponds to the (38.2%) retracement of the Aussie's drop from the late September high (~$0.6940) to that early February low. Yet, the momentum indicators suggest the upside correction is advanced and likely nearly over. A close back below $.6330 may be an early confirmation.

Mexico

Drivers: The US tariff threat looms on the horizon, and the central bank of Mexico has kept the door open to another 50 bp cut next month. The swaps market is discounting about 100 bp of cuts over the next six months. The market was encouraged by softer than expected January CPI. Moreover, the estimate for the second half of last month suggests price pressures are still moderating. President Sheinbaum response to the US and the tariff threat has won praise in Mexico, even among her critics.

Data: With Q4 GDP already reported, December retail sales are unlikely to move the market. The minutes from the recent Banxico meeting are due. Slowing growth and inflation back within target are encouraging officials to reduce the restrictiveness of monetary policy. However, the central bank does not meet again until March 27. By that time, the US tariff picture may be clearer, and more data will be available.

Prices: The Mexican peso rose by about 1.25% last week, its first back-to-back weekly gain of the year. The US dollar fell to MXN20.2625 ahead of the weekend in its lowest level since January 24, when it briefly traded slightly below MXN20.1350. It has not traded below MXN20.00 since last November. Since the spike high on February 3 (~MXN21.2930), the greenback has depreciated by about 4.85%. The momentum indicators are consistent with limited near-term downside.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: Shrinking Earnings Offset By Increased Investment Activity
data.description:
Overview

Gladstone Investment (NASDAQ:GAIN) operates as a business development company that aims to generate attractive total returns through its portfolio of debt investments. What makes GAIN unique compared to other BDCs is that its portfolio has a meaningful allocation towards equity focused investments. I previously covered GAIN and issued a hold rating due to its weakening dividend coverage and rise in non-accruals. I believed that the portfolio was becoming increasingly vulnerable to the prolonged elevated interest rate environment. Now that the interest rate outlook has shifted since the time of my last coverage, I wanted to revisit GAIN to provide some updated insights following their latest quarterly earnings.

Looking over the last twelve months, we can see that GAIN has underperformed the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) in both price change and total return. I like to compare a BDC's performance against BIZD since it's the closest representation that we have of an BDC tracking index. GAIN's price increased by a modest 1%, while including all distributions boosts the total return up to 13.6%. GAIN currently sports a dividend yield of nearly 7%, which makes it an attractive pick for investors seeking a high level of dividend income. However, I believe that the sustainability of this dividend has substantially weakened.

The concerns that I have with GAIN are related to the level of non-accruals within its portfolio. The expectation around the number of interest rate cuts that would take place over 2025 has been greatly reduced. Since GAIN's portfolio seems to be showing signs of weakness from the prolonged elevated interest rate environment, I worry about the overall appeal of GAIN going forward. So let's first take a look at the BDC's portfolio strategy and investment approach.

Portfolio Strategy

GAIN's portfolio is a lot more focused than traditional BDCs, as it is only exposed to three different sectors: Business/Consumer Services, Consumer Products, and Manufacturing. The portfolio at fair value amounts to $1B, representing a 25.7% increase from the prior quarter's fair value of $853M. The investments only span across 26 different portfolio companies. The very specific portfolio focus leaves GAIN vulnerable to concentration risks. If these sectors are hit with headwinds, GAIN's portfolio will be less capable of navigating it with minimal impact.

Based on the latest breakdown, the $1B in assets are broken down as follows:

  1. Secured First Lien Debt: $599.9M - (56.2%)
  2. Secured Second Lien Debt: $108.7M - (10.2%)
  3. Preferred Equity: $308.2M - (28.9)
  4. Common Equity: $50.3M - (4.7%)

As we can see, traditional first and second lien debt investments make up approximately 66% of the portfolio. The remaining 34% is made up of preferred and common equity-based investments. The first and second lien debt investments offer more stability and less risk since they sit higher up on the corporate capital structure and have a greater priority for repayment. This helps reduce the probability that GAIN loses all invested capital in a bad transaction. If an underlying borrower goes through a default and has to liquidate assets, GAIN would be entitled to repayment before other types of debt.

However, we can see that over a third of the portfolio is structured as equity investments. Equity investments sit on the very bottom of the capital structure and have the lowest priority for repayment, which means they are generally considered the highest risk. Equity investments made not produce consistent cash flows like traditional secured debt investments, but they have the potential to allow for a greater amount of capital appreciation over time through growth of the underlying borrower.

Q3 Earnings & Risk Profile

GAIN recently reported their Q3 earnings and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.23 per share, missing expectations by $0.1. Additionally, total investment income landed at $21.3M, representing a year-over-year decrease of 7.4%. It's also worth mentioning that total expenses also increased by 32.3% from last quarter, now amounting to $20.2M. The increases in expenses resulted in a much lower net investment income of only $1.16M, representing a massive drop of 84.1% from the previous quarter. Management touched on this during the latest earnings call by attributing the increased costs to incentive fees.

This increase was primarily due to $5.8 million increase in capital gains based incentive fees resulting from the positive net impact of realized and unrealized gains and losses during the quarter and accrued as required under the US GAAP. In addition to a $1.3 million increase in our income based incentive fee. These expense increases were partially offset by a $2.2 million increase in credits to fees from Adviser, which were driven by the significant investment activity - Taylor Ritchie, CFO

Despite the decrease in total investment income, the net increase in net assets from operations amounted to $1.05 per share. This contributed to the NAV growing to $13.30 per share, up from the prior quarter's NAV of $12.49 per share. A growing NAV is the clearest indicator that GAIN's portfolio strategy is still effective at generating returns. While the short-term challenges should still be acknowledged and monitored, GAIN's performance is still trending in the right direction.

Management has delivered meaningful strides in the rate at which they invest into new opportunities that can grow their portfolio. For instance, they've invested a significant amount of capital towards three brand-new portfolio companies, which can ultimately contribute to a growing NAV and net investment income in the future once the positions start to materialize. The portfolio activity over the quarter amounted to $182.2M, and was allocated as follows:

  • $27.2M invested into a new portfolio company, Pyrotek Special Effects. $20.1M of this was in the form of secured first lien debt, while the remaining $7.1M was in the form of preferred equity.
  • $5M invested into Gladstone Alternative Income Fund, and existing affiliate.
  • $71.3M invested into a new portfolio company, Nielsen-Kellerman. $49.1M of this was on a secured first lien basis, while the remaining $22.2M was in the form of preferred equity.
  • $78.7M invested into a new portfolio company, Ricardo Defense. $61.3M of this was on a secured first lien basis, while the remaining $17.4M was in the form of preferred equity.

When it comes to measuring the risk profile of a BDC, I like to measure the changes in non-accruals. Non-accruals represent the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. Companies that are in non-accrual status are not actively contributing to a BDC's net investment income and NAV growth. At the time of my last coverage, the rate of non-accruals sat at 13.8% of cost and 8.2% of fair portfolio value.

Reviewing their most recent form 10-Q, GAIN's non-accrual rate has slightly improved to 11.5% of cost and 7% of fair portfolio value. While seeing an improvement here is nice, the non-accrual rates still remain abnormally high when compared against some peer BDCs. I guess this can be partially explained by GAIN's much smaller portfolio, but the higher rate of non-accruals make me a bit nervous.

I will continue to monitor for changes and would like to see these non-accruals continually trend downward over the course of 2025. While non-accruals aren't the entire picture, we can see how GAIN has underperformed these peers. For reference, here are the rate of non-accruals for peer BDCs and their respective performance over the last twelve months.

  • Gladstone Capital (GLAD): 4% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN): 1.4% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Updated Valuation

Since GAIN operates as a BDC, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its net assets. Looking over its ten-year history, we can see that the price of GAIN has frequently shifted between the discount and premium territory. The price currently trades at a modest premium to NAV of about 4%. For reference, GAIN's price has traded at an average premium to NAV of 4.4% over the last three-year period. Therefore, GAIN sits somewhere around its fair valuation at this time. However, I think that the price can retract and cause it to dip into the discount territory as long as we remain in a higher interest rate environment.

When comparing the federal funds rate to GAIN's price, we can see a bit of an inverse relationship. When rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the 2020 pandemic, GAIN's price rapidly grew. An environment of lower interest rates catered to a growth environment as there were more businesses seeking debt financing. This provided an abundance of opportunities for GAIN to capitalize on and grow their portfolio. Conversely, the price of GAIN retracted to the downside and eventually traded sideways as rates were hiked to their decade high. A higher rate environment is a less favorable environment for new borrowers, meaning there are less opportunities for GAIN to grow its portfolio.

While higher rates may be great for income generation, it puts additional strain on borrowers. This is exactly why we've seen GAIN's non-accrual rate balloon so high over the last two years. The original expectations going into 2025 was that there would be at least four different interest rate cut to take place. However, the Fed has become a lot more hawkish on their stance and now only estimates there to be one interest rate cut to take place this year. Depending on the inflationary data over the year, there's even a chance that rates are increased at some point.

Now that the outlook assumes less rate cuts to happen, GAIN's portfolio may continue to feel the weight of this increased pressure. This makes it entirely possible that non-accruals start to rise again. Therefore, GAIN's portfolio health is something that I plan to continuously monitor over the next several quarters. Wall St. currently has an average price target of $13.80 per share, indicating a slight downside of 0.7%. I tend to agree with this stance, since the outlook has become less favorable for GAIN.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits at about 7.2%. If we multiply this monthly payout by 3 to get a quarterly conversion, we get a quarter distribution amount of $0.24 per share. As previously mentioned, adjusted net investment income landed at $0.23 per share for the quarter. This indicates that net investment income no longer fully supports the monthly payouts at the moment. During the earnings call, management stated that they have confidence in the ability to maintain the annual distribution amount of $0.96 per share, thanks to their strong balance sheet.

We believe the state of our portfolio is very good. It's growing. We have a strong and liquid balance sheet and a high level of buyout activity and the prospect of continued very good earnings and distributions over this next year. - Dave Dullum, President

However, I remain a bit cautious since any further impacts to the rate of non-accruals can continue to negative impact their net investment income levels. It seems like management is betting on their buyout strategy to continue to produce excess capital, as it has done in the past. The buyout strategy has fueled large supplemental distributions in the past, but I would much rather have baseline coverage from existing positions, as it would instill more confidence as an investor.

Any additional deterioration in existing positions or one bad transaction, and it can result in a dividend cut. However, I could fully understand if readers are more optimistic than I am. After all, GAIN did make large investments during the quarter, which can lead to a higher earnings rate once these materialize over the next several quarters. I would assume that this is why management hasn't already cut the dividend. Therefore, I will be maintaining my position but closely monitoring performance over the next several quarters. If their buyout strategies yield large supplementals like it has in the past, I would hate to miss out on it. I will not be reinvesting my distributions, but I will instead allocate these dollars to other areas of my portfolio that offer a better value proposition.

Takeaway

In conclusion, GAIN's recent earnings were a bit of a mixed bag. Net investment income slightly decreased, but the NAV did increase quarter over quarter. Despite net investment income no longer covering the base distribution, management seems confident in their ability to continue to support base distributions going forward as a result of their new investments and focused buyout strategies that can yield positive results. Non-accruals may remain abnormally high, but they are trending in a positive direction. The lack of future rate cuts could be a challenge going forward, so I plan to continue monitoring the non-accrual rates and overall portfolio health over the next several quarters. The price trades at a reasonable valuation in terms of price to NAV, so if results are positive going forward, this could be a solid entry point.

data.title: SailPoint: Sailing Back Into The Public Market
data.description:
SailPoint, Inc. (NASDAQ:SAIL) is the newest entrant into the public market. As a leader in unified identity security for enterprises, the company raised $1.38 billion in an upsized IPO, ultimately pricing at $23/share. The company sold 57.5 million shares to the public, with an additional 2.5 million shares being sold by an existing shareholder (Thoma Bravo). The company's new valuation is ~$12.6 billion.

For investors familiar with SailPoint, this will be the company’s second time going through an IPO, with the first entrance of a public company occurring in 2017. After a successful IPO, the company experienced a strong period of growth, with security software names in general experiencing a bull market. This ultimately led to Thoma Bravo acquiring SailPoint for $6.9 billion.

Fast forward to today, SailPoint has since launched several offerings to fortify its identity security and governance portfolio. Essentially, SailPoint helps large enterprises manage and secure user access to systems, applications, and data. Some main areas of focus include:

  • Identity governance: Ensuring the right users have the right access to the right credentials
  • Access management: Controlling and automating user access based on company policies and compliance restrictions
  • Privileged access management: Securing high-risk accounts with more in-depth permission controls
  • Cloud identity security: Managing identities across cloud platforms, including the main hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)
  • AI-driven insights: Providing identity insights to detect potential risks and optimize access policies

In an ever-changing landscape around access management, enterprises across the world are heavily investing into their security protocols and access management.

Since going private back in 2017, Thoma Bravo and SailPoint have heavily invested into the company’s solutions, including the launch of the Atlas Platform as well as multiple tuck-in acquisitions. The Atlas unified architecture platform is the unified architecture that spans across the platform, providing for dep integration capabilities. On top of that, the company’s Identity Cube provides a complete view of an enterprise’s identity and access management.

SailPoint has also embedded AI-powered features throughout the platform, which provides companies with even greater visibility into its access management and governance.

The company’s CEO is Mark McClain, who co-founded the company back in 2005 and has spearheaded all of its innovation. Coming back into the public market with an experienced, IPO-ready CEO could be viewed as a material advantage for investors looking to capitalize on a fast-growing segment of the broader security market.

Uncovering the Financials

One very important item to understand with SailPoint is that the company has undergone a SaaS model transition. What that means is that the company stopped selling its product on a License basis – which is often volatile and recognized as mostly up-front revenue – and moved in favor of Subscription revenue – which is more recurring.

From a customer standpoint, the experience can often be better with Subscription products because the company, in this case SailPoint, takes ownership of the product and is responsible for upkeeping the back-end. The only technical requirement from the customer’s standpoint is during the integration process. Otherwise, SailPoint is responsible for constantly updating the product and maintaining the current version.

Think of a Netflix subscription. Rather than paying $144 for a yearly subscription, Netflix charges customers $12/month. This model provides the company with higher visibility of its recurring revenue.

For the nine months ending October 31, 2024, SailPoint experienced total revenue growth of 25% yoy, an impressive feat. Importantly, Subscription revenue now accounts for 92% of total revenue, up from 88% in the year-ago period. Subscription revenue growth was 30% during this same time period.

As seen in the chart above, Perpetual License revenue is almost at $0, meaning the revenue headwind from this model transition is now essentially over.

That is why I believe it is essential investors look at both Subscription revenue growth and ARR growth. When looking at ARR, growth was 30% during the most recent quarter, reaching $813 million. In fact, growth has consistently settled into the 29-32% range over the past four quarters.

Another important metric to follow is the dollar-based net retention rate – or the company’s ability to sell additional products to the same customer. As seen above, SailPoint has delivered a consistent ~114% dollar-based net retention rate over the past five quarters. Although the longer-term trend has improved from ~108%, I would be hesitant to believe the pace of improvement can continue. One of the reasons behind this improvement was the several product and platform investments made during the company’s time under Thoma Bravo.

SailPoint predominately focuses on selling to large enterprises, as demonstrated by its 2,895 total customer base, which includes 50% of the Fortune 500 and 25% of the Forbes Global 2000. The focus on large enterprises can serve SailPoint very well, as this customer cohort tends to be stickier, churn less, and has the propensity to spend more over time. In fact, SailPoint saw a 67% yoy growth in customers with >$1 million in ARR.

When looking at non-GAAP financial metrics, the company’s gross margin has steadily climbed closer to 80%, which is being driven by Subscription gross margin of ~84%. While the mix of Subscription revenue is likely to modestly increase over time given its faster growth, gross margin of low-80’s could be the ceiling, which is a common occurrence for leading software companies.

Non-GAAP operating margins have also improved materially in recent years, going from a non-GAAP operating loss to a ~14% margin for the nine-month period ending October 31, 2024. Over time, operating margins are likely to expand given the company’s ongoing efforts to improve efficiency. While margins may come under near-term as SailPoint adopts public company-related expenses, the ~14% margin is a strong starting point.

Valuation

Given the company only recently went public, it may take some time before consensus estimates start to build in. However, we can synthesize potential growth rates based on the company’s historical achievements. Management estimates its TAM to be $55 billion, which is driven by the increasing awareness of identity-related security breaches and cyber-attacks. Other expert reports estimate the Identity Access Management market to be growing anywhere from 13% (source) to 15% (source).

Given SailPoint’s strength in the market, it would be reasonable to assume growth remains above the 20% level for quite some time. In fact, SailPoint was recently recognized in a 2024 Gartner report for Identity Governance and Administration, further demonstrating the company’s industry accolades.

Post-IPO, the company plans to use some proceeds to pay down debt. On a pro forma basis, the company estimates total cash to be $308 million and total debt to be $345 million. The company also expects to have roughly 546.6 million shares.

In the chart above, I assume various growth three-year CAGR’s based off the current full-year revenue trajectory of ~$875 million. Additionally, this matrix includes several valuation multiples. Software companies often trade on revenue multiples, given its fast-growth nature. It is not uncommon for leading software companies to trade above 10x revenue, with more challenged companies trading in the 2-4x range.

Given the current stock price ~$23, this would imply a steady 20% revenue growth over the next year and a ~8x revenue multiple, a very reasonable multiple to pay for a company growing 20% with operating margins expanding from a 14% baseline level.

If revenue growth were to accelerate closer to 30%, it is not unreasonable to see this stock trade closer to $40, a material upside to current levels. Yes, this would take a lot of strong execution and some multiple expansion; however, it illustrates the upside potential here.

For now, I remain bullish on SailPoint. The combination of the long-term growth opportunity, ongoing support from Thoma Bravo, improvements made during its short tenure as a private company, and focus on improving profitability make this an attractive investment.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to SailPoint is its early performance as a recent IPO. Typically, software IPOs have high expectations in the year following an IPO. Investors expect management to provide conservative guidance and then execute well above that level. Particularly with higher valuation companies, the room for error is much lower. If SailPoint were to disappoint on one of its quarterly reports over the next year, the stock’s valuation may suffer.

Additionally, SailPoint competes in a challenging market. Other leading companies in this space include Okta (OKTA), CyberArk (CYBR), One Identity, and other larger companies such as IBM (IBM), Microsoft (MSFT), and Oracle (ORCL). Increased competition may lead to customer churn and/or pricing pressure.

data.title: Qualcomm Stock's Setup Is Attractive (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

Since my buy rating on QUALCOMM Inc. (NASDAQ:QCOM) (NEOE:QCOM:CA) late last year, the stock has gained around 6%. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals are bullish, with the stock entering into an uptrend and the moving averages generally showing bullish signs. For the fundamentals, the business is continuing to perform rather well, but guidance shows that revenue growth may decelerate in the coming quarter. Nonetheless, the stock currently seems modestly undervalued as the P/E ratio's massive discount to peers is unjustified with Qualcomm poised to be a key player in the edge AI space. Therefore, since the technicals are bullish and the stock seems undervalued despite some growth deceleration, I reiterate my buy rating on Qualcomm stock.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Qualcomm stock entered into an uptrend, starting in late December. The trend line has already acted as support on many occasions, and as recent as earlier this month. Therefore, expect this trend line to continue to be support moving forward. Prior to this uptrend forming, there was a bit of a downtrend in the stock, and so there has been a significant reversal.

Support

In addition to the uptrend line, there are two other support levels beneath the stock. The nearer one is at 165 and this area seems to have been a key area throughout the last six months as it has acted as both resistance and support multiple times. The other support area is at 160 and represents the nearest unfilled upside gap in the stock.

Resistance

There are two major resistance levels above the stock, and both are quite near. The first is at 173 and is a recent downside gap. The stock seems to be experiencing some resistance there currently. The other resistance level is in the mid-170s. This area was resistance in late August, September, late January, and earlier this month as well.

Takeaway

The message conveyed by this chart is net positive in my view. While the stock has both strong support and resistance levels, it has entered into an uptrend and so further gains is the more likely scenario at this point.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA had a bearish death cross with the 200-day SMA back in September. The gap between the SMAs widened significantly from November to mid-January but has been shrinking in the past month or so, showing that longer term bearish momentum is receding. The stock currently trades between the SMAs, and the 200-day SMA looks like it has acted as resistance lately.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA in early January, a promising near term signal. However, the gap between the EMAs has shrunken a bit as of late, reflecting a decrease in near term bullish momentum. The stock still trades above both EMAs and so there is hope for a re-acceleration in momentum.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke above the midline to start the new year, a signal of strength in the stock, and it also helps to confirm the validity of the uptrend. The midline has since acted as support, and so the bulls have clearly been resilient lately. Currently, the Bollinger Bands are in a volatility contraction phase but given the state of things, a positive volatility breakout is more likely moving forward.

Takeaway

Overall, I believe these moving averages show a strong technical outlook for Qualcomm stock. The SMAs show receding long-term bearishness, the EMAs are still in a bullish state, and the Bollinger Bands confirm the strength of the uptrend.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD had a bearish crossover with the signal line earlier this month, but the gap between the lines has remained nearly non-existent, as shown by the flat histogram. This shows that bearish momentum has been highly contained despite the crossover. The MACD also displays long-term strength as it recently made a six-month high despite the stock being unable to best its high seen back in November. This shows that strength is building and the stock has room to run.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

The RSI currently has a reading of 55.51 after it declined from a near 70 level earlier this month. While this shows bullishness has decreased, the bulls are nonetheless still in control of the stock. Like the MACD, the RSI also shows long-term strength, as it also made a six-month high in mid-January and was much higher than back at the November peak.

Stochastics

The %K and %D lines are virtually at the same level right now. The stochastics recently had a bullish crossover, but the %K line was not able to pull away and widen the gap significantly. Instead, that gap has closed, showing that there is some immediate weakness in the bulls currently. Both lines are also right at the 50 level, and so overall, this indicator shows uncertainty in the stock's next move. For the longer run, the stochastics also shows strength as it too made a six-month high in January.

Takeaway

I would say that these indicators show a very slightly net positive near term outlook for Qualcomm stock. The MACD shows contained bearishness, the RSI shows that the bulls remain in control of the stock, and the stochastics show uncertainty. Overall, the message here is mixed and reflects indecision. However, for the longer term, there is clearly strength building as all three indicators made major highs in January.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Qualcomm released their FY2025 Q1 results on Feb 5 and showed resilient results overall. They reported revenues of $11.669 billion, up 17% YoY and a diluted GAAP EPS of $2.83, up 15% YoY. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth continues to be at healthy levels and is at slightly above average levels compared to the past three years. Despite some minor slowdown from the previous quarter, revenue growth is still on a good trend since late 2023. For GAAP EPS growth, there has been a significant slowdown in growth since the past quarter, but compared to the past three years of EPS growth figures, Q1's results are actually not bad and are at around average levels. In terms of non-GAAP, they did report an adjusted diluted EPS of $3.41, up 24% YoY. All of these results also beat expectations with revenue beating by $731.80 million, GAAP EPS beating by $0.39 and adjusted EPS beating by $0.42, reflecting strong performance versus consensus projections.

Guidance

Qualcomm provided guidance figures for their FY2025 Q2. They are expecting GAAP revenues of between $10.3 billion and $11.2 billion. Since GAAP revenues were $9.389 billion in FY2024 Q2, this guidance represents revenue growth ranging from 9.7% to 19.3%. At the midpoint, these growth estimates represent a minor slowdown in growth versus their Q1. They also project that they will have a GAAP diluted EPS of between $2.38 and $2.58. GAAP diluted EPS was $2.06 in FY2024 Q2, and so they expect GAAP EPS growth ranging from 15.5% to 25.2%. At the midpoint, this would be a slight acceleration in GAAP EPS growth from their Q1. Lastly, they are expecting an adjusted diluted EPS of between $2.70 and $2.90 for FY2025 Q2 and since this figure was $2.44 in FY2024 Q2, adjusted diluted EPS growth is expected to fall between 10.7% and 18.9%. At the midpoint, it would be a considerable slowdown from Q1's 24% growth rate. Overall, I believe Qualcomm's guidance was mixed and a bit weak as revenue growth is expected to slow modestly and while GAAP EPS growth may see some improvement, adjusted EPS growth deceleration could be concerning.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio currently looks to be at slightly above average levels of the past three years and has a reading of 4.760 as of now. It has receded from the mid-2024 high levels of above 6.0. I believe this ratio shows around fair valuation for the stock currently. As discussed above, revenue growth for Q1 seems to be at slightly above average levels currently and so there is no major disconnect between the ratio and financial growth. Furthermore, while guidance shows that revenue growth may slow for Q2, it is likely to be a modest decrease that won't change the conclusions from this analysis by a significant amount. Currently, the Information Technology sector's median P/S ratio is 3.36 and so Qualcomm is trading at a moderate premium to peers. In my view, this is not to be unexpected due to Qualcomm's strong position in edge AI semiconductors.

Price To Earnings Ratio (P/E)

The P/E ratio seems to be at around average levels currently compared with the past few years. Its reading of 18.33 currently, is far below the levels seen in mid-2024 of over 30. I believe this ratio also shows fair valuation for Qualcomm stock relative to financial growth. As discussed above, GAAP EPS growth is currently at around average levels and the guidance that they provided shows that there will be a modest acceleration in this figure for Q2. Therefore, in my view, this ratio is tracking EPS growth rather well and shows an overall correct valuation relative to the financials. However, the IT sector's median P/E ratio is currently 33.03 and so Qualcomm is actually trading at a significant discount to peers according to this measure. I do not believe this is justified, as Qualcomm should be a key beneficiary of edge AI tailwinds. Therefore, I believe that Qualcomm is significantly undervalued versus the sector and could still experience multiple expansion despite unspectacular growth in its financials.

Takeaway

I conclude that Qualcomm is modestly undervalued at current levels. The P/S ratio generally reflects things quite well, as it tracks revenue growth sufficiently and is at an appropriate premium to the sector. While the P/E also tracks EPS growth quite well, the massive discount to the IT sector is unwarranted as Qualcomm is set to see significant tailwinds from edge AI developments. Therefore, I believe some multiple expansion is not out of the question by any means.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are very strong for Qualcomm stock despite some mixed signals in the indicators, with the chart and moving averages showing promising indications. The fundamentals are overall strong as well, but guidance was mixed for their Q2. Despite some potential growth deceleration, I believe Qualcomm is currently modestly undervalued as it trades at a massive P/E discount to the IT sector. Its leadership position in edge AI is potentially a significant tailwind that could drive the company's growth in the decades to come. Therefore, since the technicals are strong and the fundamental setup is overall attractive, I reiterate my buy rating on Qualcomm stock.
